I'm the best boss.

08:18
Vrijen Attawar
Because obviously, you know, by definition that's. That's the thing with

Logan
So we've. We were working like on the kind of company that we founded
together, which is more consultancy, for a few years in Singapore. We
tried to scale that. We kept being like, hey, it should be possible to
kind of do this thing, which is essentially just asking good questions
and showing people a mirror, to be like, this is what I heard and what
you just said. And people were like, wow, you think I'm. You think I can
do that or you think I. I didn't even think of that skill. And it was
this kind of motion of helping people build a lot of confidence just by
kind of sharing what we learned about them and, you know, probing a
little bit more. And so were mostly working with younger people at that
time, kind of like ages 16 to 26.

09:05
Logan
That motion of, you know, who am I? What am I good at? What do I want to
do? And I think that is for anybody who's had kind of like a nonlinear
career or is like a multi hyphenate that's becoming more and more, I
would say, common for a lot of people to have those career inflection
points to be like, hey, let me take a step back.

09:22
Vrijen Attawar
Who.

09:23
Logan
Who am I? What am I good at? What should I be doing? And I think that is
essentially where we came back together two and a half years ago around
essentially the Same. The same question or the same problem of how can
people get support in moments of, you know, career transition, whether
that's a pivot, whether that's trying to move up, whether that's kind of
like, how can I really grab all my assets together and do something with
them? So we've been working on career span for, yeah, two and a half
years, which I've just. I've aged. I've aged a thousand years in that
time, I think. But as with any startup, you know, but we started around
kind of the candidate experience very much centered on job seekers and
trying to help them with positioning and storytelling at the core.

10:08
Logan
Like, not, you know, just another resume builder. But like, okay, like,
that's almost window dressing. Like, you got to figure out what it is
that your story is and how you can feel confident in. In kind of
communicating that to the right audience in the right way. And that kind
of career, it was always centered around conversations, and that's what
the platform is still very much based on. And it's, you know, audio
input. You can kind of just talk stream of consciousness and it kind of
reflects back, you know, what it has learned about you. And that kind of
builds out almost like a mental model of your professional experiences.
We kind of call it the 3D resume in the sense that it is digging deeper
into those experiences beyond the bullet point.

10:46
Logan
And so, you know, were playing around with mostly B2C, I'd say, for the
first, like, year and a half and really getting the tech right. And in
that time, a lot has changed since we started, obviously, both with AI,
both with the hiring space. And the last, like, six months in
particular, I think, have been a real. A real shift in the tides in
terms of, like, how people are trying to think beyond the resume. And so
basically, and I'll pause here, but, you know, what we've been doing in
the last few months in particular is saying, like, okay, we are
capturing a lot of phenomenal, really nuanced information from the
candidate side that could be of use on the employer side as well, and
kind of also working with.

11:27
Logan
With communities who want to bring more kind of resources to their
members, which is. Which is a lot to throw at you, I know, but, like,
that's essentially, you know, where we are right now is that we've built
a product that is very beloved by job seekers, and now we're bringing
opportunities to those job seekers and by partnering with employers and
kind offering it communities as well. And so, yeah, that's kind of where
we are But I've got no bridge. Probably got a whole bunch of questions
lined up, but we can kind of pause there and see if.

11:56
Vrijen Attawar
Yeah, let's start by answering yours. Yeah, if you have any.

12:00
Krista Tan
Yeah, I mean a million, of course. How were you monetizing when you were
just doing the B2C side?

12:08
Logan
We were three months a three month subscription for 50 bucks or $49. So
end of the day, like as you can imagine, monetizing on the B2C side was
always going to be like threading the needle. And so what we had done
for about a year and we still have a few existing partnerships was going
through higher ed and through the university route by partnering with
career services like graduate programs. I think the macro there
obviously also really changed with higher ed and budgets and the
administration and everything like that. And it was going to be, we
still have some pilot, some paid programs with Columbia, with UMass,
with a few of the other schools that will be essentially just I think a
multi year outcomes driven, like how can we measure impact over time?
Which is a very higher ed play.

12:55
Logan
So it's kind of like we've set those up, those will continue. Those are
kind of the B2U like monetized side of things, but that will be a longer
term play. So yeah, we started right now the product is free for users,
like so for B2C it's completely free. We will be introducing a kind of
freemium model where it's up until a certain amount of usage, then you
pay kind of like OpenAI style, like you can pay 10 bucks a month kind of
thing if once you pass a certain level of usage.

13:22
Vrijen Attawar
Yeah, yeah.

13:23
Krista Tan
And what does usage mean? Like the seeing your profile now or what is
that?

13:30
Vrijen Attawar
So to be super specific and to divide it into end users and sort of the
companies for the end user, the candidate, all of the coaching
conversations are free and always will be. There's a cap on the number
of resumes you can generate, but we're lifting that cap pretty sort of
like progressively. And most folks don't hit the cap. And then there's
unlimited vibe checks.

13:55
Vrijen Attawar
What we call vibe checks are essentially just testing you against the
job description to get a sense of not just like would you pass the ats,
which is a whole different, you know, whether that's even a legitimate
strategy or not now is a question, but it actually looks at the
underlying meaning of what kind of person would be good for this role
and do you being the kind of person you are with the achievements and
values and stuff that you have, does that line up with you? So it's a
much deeper, much meatier sort of analysis and came from a place of, you
know, can we help folks waste less time or get less emotionally attached
to jobs? Or at the very least can.

14:35
Vrijen Attawar
If they are going for a job where they, you know, are unsuccessful, they
at least have a modicum of feedback or a clue as to why they may not
have been picked. Right. If only for closure's sake. So I think that is
what we've, per Logan's point, built is a very robust, highly effective
consumer product that we are, you know, crazy approach here, giving away
for free, essentially, because what we believe is far more valuable is
not only giving people access to that kind of capability and confidence
building for free, but also using that to hone in on the best candidates
and surface them to employers in a way that provides a supplement to the
pipelines that they currently use that are of varying degrees of signal.

15:23
Krista Tan
Okay, interesting. I could probably go on with a million more questions,
but I know you all have an agenda here today too, so let's get to your
questions and then I guess I'll speckle in some more questions if I need
clarification.

Vrijen Attawar
Sure. Yeah, so. So the first one I sort of wanted to just get a sense of

interest in a vendor or an external. Like, what is the kind of value
that they deserve that you go. That they deliver? That makes you go, oh,
actually I want to listen to this. This actually cuts through the crap
that normally comes across my desk.

16:19
Krista Tan
When you say vendor, what do you mean by that?

16:22
Vrijen Attawar
I guess just any external partner, right. Whether that is another

your community.

16:35
Krista Tan
For us, a couple of things come to mind. One is the quality of the

know, like, I just don't see a future for you, honestly. And so, like,
we're probably not going to feature that tool inside our community. Two

values in this alignment with some partners that don't really see like
the long term value of relationship building. They just want to see like
instant gratification, instant results. Like if you can't give me a
number of my roi at the end of this webinar, we don't want to partner
with you.

17:30
Krista Tan
And it's like, great, let's part ways because we're more about like a
long term relationship and building that and that the partners that we

17:43
Logan
Right.

17:45
Krista Tan
So those are some initial things I would point to. I'm sure there's

17:55
Logan
Yeah, that totally makes sense. What do you see like in terms of, for

like I know that's a big question, but like where do you see it? Like
you already have like 10 chapters. Like it's so cool like how it's
built, how it's grown and where do you want to kind of focus your
efforts within the community building side of it? Is it more on the
member experience? Is it expansion? Like what, where, what's on your
agenda in the next like six to 12 months?

18:24
Krista Tan
Yeah. Both my co founder and I just recently got full time jobs. We
bootstrapped this two years in, have both just gone through our savings
and we, you know, both just kind of came to like a come to Jesus for

right now. So we're not as focused on like growth at the costs. I think

chapters that great. I don't think that will happen because naturally we
now have this snowball effect happening. And like I literally just got
off a call with another person that wants to start a new chapter for us
and you know, just kind of like word of mouth just expands.

19:12
Krista Tan
I think that's a big part of community building is like the first couple
of years you almost can't force the growth too much because a lot of it
is word of mouth and that kind of stuff just takes time. So I think we
will continue to grow, but we're not like, yeah, have to grow. Right.
The one thing that's also coming to mind is like the quality of the
programming that we offer to our members is really important to us. It's
more about quality than quantity. Kind of had to figure that out the
hard way. Like I'm a very ambitious person and I vote. So my co founder
and I split responsibilities. So I've always done like our member
engagement. I do our virtual programming.

19:56
Krista Tan
So like all of our like workshops and fireside chats and we have pure

week. Like I would just like pile them in because I was meeting so many
great speakers and it was just like too much for our. So really had to
scale back on programming because I've realized like, you know, it's
like decision fatigue for people. They're like, I have too many things
to choose from. I'm not gonna go totally.

20:30
Vrijen Attawar
No. A light touches is so key with this stuff. You're so right.

20:34
Krista Tan
And they have to be really good. Like if someone comes to a workshop,
they're like, this hour needs to be worth it to take it away from my
full time job or what have you. So focus more on quality now, I think.

20:46
Vrijen Attawar
Right. Another question that I have is what is the like when it comes to

Logan
Well.

21:15
Krista Tan
Okay, I guess I'll just start by saying because our community is for

Right.

Krista Tan
Yeah. So job seekers, I mean they want connections to people that are

I only want to join your membership to get a job. It's like this isn't

22:22
Krista Tan
Here to do, like, if you want to contribute to a larger good and want to

23:03
Vrijen Attawar
Yeah. And one very important thing that sort of occurs to me based on

step back, I was especially interested in that because I think that the
behavior of the folks that are on both sides of the process is obviously

relationship you want, whether it's with your community members or with
vendors partners, is one where the thing to do is to work towards
something constructive and meaningful. And all of the other stuff will
come inevitably as a result of engaging with sort of constructive
intent. And that's totally where we are as well. Right.

23:50
Vrijen Attawar
We almost tell people like, you know, I've gotten used to telling people
the. We work on our careers at the exact wrong time. Right. We try to
like build a network when we should have a warm network. We try to write
our resume when we've emotionally checked out of like our job and don't
want to think about it. You know, it's those, those sort of ironies of
life. And the way that we want to shift folks is to move them to a world
where they engage with a tool like Careerspan on an ongoing basis. You
know, you had a win this month. Great drop in a story, right. Very easy
to sort of store and use and you know, sort of to maintain an in depth
record of your achievements, but in a very human centric sort of way.

24:32
Vrijen Attawar
And then making the job hunt process as easy as, you know what, Maybe I
like, I'm not ready to announce that I'm looking, but I just kind of
want to see what's out there. Click a button. Tailored jobs get
recommended to you. Toggle another button. Okay, great. You're ready for

thing that personally motivated us in this direction was a comment in
the Next Play community channel where I did a survey about delivering
jobs to people and someone said, if you will give me a warm intro to a
company, I'll pay you anything you want.

25:05
Krista Tan
Right, Totally.

25:07
Vrijen Attawar
That's, that's it. That's. That's. That's the name of the game. And I
give Logan a ton of credit. She, like, almost a year back, sort of honed
in on that fact and we've sort of been kind of coming back to that and
now I think what we want to do is be this sort of like middle signal
alternative to referrals, which are very high signal, but a trickle.
Right. If you're lucky, and a very low signal inbound channel. And I
guess on that point I sort of wanted to get more. So your thoughts as a
talent professional, like, do you think there exists a space for
something that, you know, provides more talent to you? Sure. But in this
very concise, shortlisted, reliably provenance sort of way.

25:53
Krista Tan
Yeah. From the client of the company, the hiring company side.

25:57
Vrijen Attawar
That's right. Because, you know, it's coming from these great

into.

26:03
Krista Tan
Yeah. Yes, definitely. My, like, I immediately think of the challenges

there's a million tools out there trying to sell something similar. I

biggest challenge for any company in your space.

26:44
Logan
Mm.

26:45
Vrijen Attawar
Is the. Is the best thing to do in that situation or is there a better

27:05
Krista Tan
I think that's the approach you will need to take. I think it will still

There was something else I was going to say. Oh, are you thinking of,

Logan
We're right now focused around like product management and like

yeah, that's. Those are the two initial ones we're trying to niche down,

Vrijen Attawar
So.

27:55
Logan
Yeah, still broad.

Yeah, I think that's critical because they give you. If they give you a

Logan
Yeah.

Right, right.

Vrijen Attawar
If you want to, if you want to bust out some really sort of great hot

Krista Tan
Oh, we don't have time for that. We only have five minutes. The bathroom

I just told you I'VE been like, I'm like, get a full time job. Right,

Vrijen Attawar
One last thing I'd like to sort of sneak in at least sort of. One of the

professionals with a community of talent professionals had, did you

30:53
Krista Tan
I mean, so many people have said that to us and clearly we're recruiters

that was an immediate thought very early on. It was like, that feels
like a little bit of a conflict of interest. Let's just build the
community for the sake of community and see what comes of it. So never
say never. I think that could come in the future. Like, we've thought
about specifically monetizing recruiter placements, right. Because now
we have this pool of amazing talent, but I don't think the market's
right for that. Right. There's like recruiting, hiring isn't strong
enough yet. And we almost didn't want to distract ourselves from like
our main goal. So that was primary, the primary reason.

31:43
Vrijen Attawar
Gotcha. I, I really appreciate that and honestly respect the absolute

You know, we'd love for someone on your side to just check us out, sort
